html:
𐤉𐤄𐤅𐤄

THE ANCIENT COVENANT RECORD

Volume Twenty-Nine — Part One

The Book of Daniyel

DANIYEL

Prophecy — The Covenant Faithful in Exile

Chapters 1 through 6

Daniyel Resolved Not to Defile Himself — Our Creator Is Able to Deliver Us But If Not We Will Not Serve Your Gods — You Have Been Weighed in the Balances and Found Wanting — My Creator Sent His Angel

PRIMARY MANUSCRIPT AUTHORITIES

Dead Sea Scrolls — 1QDan-a — 1QDan-b — 4QDan-a — 4QDan-b — 4QDan-c — 4QDan-d — 4QDan-e — pap6QDan

Ethiopian Orit Ge'ez — Complete Canon

Masoretic Text

YHWH and The Creator only

COMPARATIVE NOTES AT END OF EVERY CHAPTER

DSS · ORIT GE'EZ · MASORETIC VARIANT · CRITICAL NOTE

YHWH / 𐤉𐤄𐤅𐤄 and The Creator only — no substitute titles

All personal names in original Hebrew form throughout

DANIYEL — THE COURT NARRATIVES — CHAPTERS 1 THROUGH 6

DANIYEL — CHAPTER 1 Nevukhadnetzar Came to Yerushalayim — He Commanded the Youths to Be Brought — Daniyel Resolved Not to Defile Himself — Creator Gave Daniyel Favor — Let Us Be Tested Ten Days — Creator Gave Them Knowledge and Skill

1 In the third year of the reign of Yehoiakim king of Yehudah, Nevukhadnetzar king of Babylon came to Yerushalayim and besieged it.

2 And YHWH gave Yehoiakim king of Yehudah into his hand, with some of the vessels of the house of Creator. And he brought them to the land of Shinar, to the house of his god, and placed the vessels in the treasury of his god.

3 Then the king commanded Ashpenaz, his chief eunuch, to bring some of the people of Yisra'EL, both of the royal family and of the nobility,

4 Youths without blemish, of good appearance and skillful in all wisdom, endowed with knowledge, understanding learning, and competent to stand in the king's palace, and to teach them the literature and language of the Chaldeans.

5 The king assigned them a daily portion of the food that the king ate, and of the wine that he drank. They were to be educated for three years, and at the end of that time they were to stand before the king.

6 Among these were Daniyel, Hananyah, Misha'el, and Azaryah of the tribe of Yehudah.

7 And the chief of the eunuchs gave them names: Daniyel he called Belteshazzar, Hananyah he called Shadrakh, Misha'el he called Meshakh, and Azaryah he called Aved-Nego.

8 But Daniyel resolved that he would not defile himself with the king's food, or with the wine that he drank. Therefore he asked the chief of the eunuchs to allow him not to defile himself.

9 And Creator gave Daniyel favor and compassion in the sight of the chief of the eunuchs,

10 And the chief of the eunuchs said to Daniyel, I fear my lord the king, who assigned your food and your drink. For why should he see that you were in worse condition than the youths who are of your own age? So you would endanger my head with the king.

11 Then Daniyel said to the steward whom the chief of the eunuchs had assigned over Daniyel, Hananyah, Misha'el, and Azaryah,

12 Test your servants for ten days. Let us be given vegetables to eat and water to drink.

13 Then let our appearance and the appearance of the youths who eat the king's food be observed by you, and deal with your servants according to what you see.

14 So he listened to them in this matter, and tested them for ten days.

15 At the end of ten days it was seen that they were better in appearance and fatter in flesh than all the youths who ate the king's food.

16 So the steward took away their food and the wine they were to drink, and gave them vegetables.

17 As for these four youths, Creator gave them learning and skill in all literature and wisdom, and Daniyel had understanding in all visions and dreams.

18 At the end of the time, when the king had commanded that they should be brought in, the chief of the eunuchs brought them in before Nevukhadnetzar.

19 And the king spoke with them, and among all of them none was found like Daniyel, Hananyah, Misha'el, and Azaryah. Therefore they stood before the king.

20 And in every matter of wisdom and understanding about which the king inquired of them, he found them ten times better than all the magicians and enchanters that were in all his kingdom.

21 And Daniyel was there until the first year of King Koresh.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] The Dead Sea Scrolls preserve eight manuscripts of Daniyel from Qumran: 1QDan-a, 1QDan-b, 4QDan-a, 4QDan-b, 4QDan-c, 4QDan-d, 4QDan-e, and pap6QDan. These eight manuscripts make Daniyel one of the most extensively attested books in the DSS corpus. The manuscripts confirm both the Hebrew sections and the Aramaic sections — Chapters 2:4b through 7:28 are in Aramaic, the rest in Hebrew. Chapter 1 establishes the covenant setting: the covenant exile of the covenant faithful in Babylon.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Daniyel Chapter 1 in full. Verse 8 — Daniyel resolved that he would not defile himself with the king's food, or with the wine that he drank. The covenant resolution of Daniyel is the covenant foundation of the entire book: the covenant young man who refuses to compromise his covenant covenant identity in the covenant Babylonian court demonstrates the covenant thesis that the covenant faithful can maintain covenant integrity within covenant foreign covenant power structures.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic text preserves Chapter 1 in full. Verse 17 — Creator gave them learning and skill in all literature and wisdom, and Daniyel had understanding in all visions and dreams. The covenant divine covenant gift of covenant understanding in visions and dreams is the covenant specific covenant gift that will make Daniyel covenant indispensable to Nevukhadnetzar and every subsequent covenant foreign covenant king: the covenant ability to interpret the covenant divine covenant communications that the covenant pagan courts cannot decipher.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 2 — YHWH gave Yehoiakim king of Yehudah into his hand. The covenant theological interpretation of the covenant Babylonian conquest: it is not Nevukhadnetzar's covenant military covenant power but YHWH's covenant sovereign covenant act that delivers Yehudah into covenant exile. The covenant same covenant theological covenant interpretation runs throughout the covenant book: the covenant foreign covenant empires are instruments of YHWH's covenant sovereign covenant governance, not autonomous covenant powers operating beyond YHWH's covenant knowledge or covenant control.

DANIYEL — CHAPTER 2 Nevukhadnetzar Had Dreams — Tell Me the Dream or You Shall Be Cut in Pieces — Daniyel Made the Matter Known — You O King Saw a Great Image — A Stone Was Cut Without Hands — The Creator of Heaven Has Set Up a Kingdom — Daniyel Was Made Ruler

1 In the second year of the reign of Nevukhadnetzar, Nevukhadnetzar had dreams. His spirit was troubled, and his sleep left him.

2 Then the king commanded that the magicians, the enchanters, the sorcerers, and the Chaldeans be summoned to tell the king his dreams. So they came in and stood before the king.

3 And the king said to them, I had a dream, and my spirit is troubled to know the dream.

4 Then the Chaldeans said to the king in Aramaic, O king, live forever. Tell your servants the dream, and we will show the interpretation.

5 The king answered and said to the Chaldeans, The word from me is firm: if you do not make known to me the dream and its interpretation, you shall be torn limb from limb, and your houses shall be laid in ruins.

6 But if you show the dream and its interpretation, you shall receive from me gifts and rewards and great honor. Therefore show me the dream and its interpretation.

7 They answered a second time and said, Let the king tell his servants the dream, and we will show its interpretation.

8 The king answered and said, I know with certainty that you are trying to gain time, because you see that the word from me is firm —

9 If you do not make the dream known to me, there is but one sentence for you. You have agreed to speak lying and corrupt words before me till the times change. Therefore tell me the dream, and I shall know that you can show me its interpretation.

10 The Chaldeans answered the king and said, There is not a man on earth who can meet the king's demand, for no great and powerful king has asked such a thing of any magician or enchanter or Chaldean.

11 The thing that the king asks is difficult, and no one can show it to the king except the gods, whose dwelling is not with flesh.

12 Because of this the king was angry and very furious, and commanded that all the wise men of Babylon be destroyed.

13 So the decree went out, and the wise men were about to be killed. And they sought Daniyel and his companions, to kill them.

14 Then Daniyel replied with prudence and discretion to Ariyokh, the captain of the king's guard, who had gone out to kill the wise men of Babylon.

15 He declared to Ariyokh, the king's captain, Why is the decree of the king so urgent? Then Ariyokh made the matter known to Daniyel.

16 And Daniyel went in and requested the king to appoint him a time, that he might show the king the interpretation.

17 Then Daniyel went to his house and made the matter known to Hananyah, Misha'el, and Azaryah, his companions,

18 And told them to seek mercy from the Creator of heaven concerning this mystery, so that Daniyel and his companions might not be destroyed with the rest of the wise men of Babylon.

19 Then the mystery was revealed to Daniyel in a vision of the night. Then Daniyel blessed the Creator of heaven.

20 Daniyel answered and said: Blessed be the name of Creator from everlasting to everlasting, to whom belong wisdom and might.

21 He changes times and seasons. He removes kings and sets up kings. He gives wisdom to the wise and knowledge to those who have understanding.

22 He reveals deep and hidden things. He knows what is in the darkness, and the light dwells with him.

23 To you, O Creator of my fathers, I give thanks and praise, for you have given me wisdom and might, and have now made known to me what we asked of you, for you have made known to us the king's matter.

24 Therefore Daniyel went in to Ariyokh, whom the king had appointed to destroy the wise men of Babylon. He went and said thus to him: Do not destroy the wise men of Babylon. Bring me in before the king, and I will show the king the interpretation.

25 Then Ariyokh brought in Daniyel before the king in haste and said thus to him: I have found among the exiles from Yehudah a man who will make known to the king the interpretation.

26 The king declared to Daniyel, whose name was Belteshazzar, Are you able to make known to me the dream that I have seen and its interpretation?

27 Daniyel answered the king and said, No wise men, enchanters, magicians, or astrologers can show to the king the mystery that the king has asked,

28 But there is a Creator in heaven who reveals mysteries, and he has made known to King Nevukhadnetzar what will be in the latter days. Your dream and the visions of your head as you lay in bed are these:

29 To you, O king, as you lay in bed came thoughts of what would be after this, and he who reveals mysteries made known to you what is to be.

30 But as for me, this mystery has been revealed to me, not because of any wisdom that I have more than all the living, but in order that the interpretation may be made known to the king, and that you may know the thoughts of your mind.

31 You saw, O king, and behold, a great image. This image, mighty and of exceeding brightness, stood before you, and its appearance was frightening.

32 The head of this image was of fine gold, its chest and arms of silver, its middle and thighs of bronze,

33 Its legs of iron, its feet partly of iron and partly of clay.

34 As you looked, a stone was cut out by no human hand, and it struck the image on its feet of iron and clay, and broke them in pieces.

35 Then the iron, the clay, the bronze, the silver, and the gold, all together were broken in pieces, and became like the chaff of the summer threshing floors. And the wind carried them away, so that not a trace of them could be found. But the stone that struck the image became a great mountain and filled the whole earth.

36 This was the dream. Now we will tell the king its interpretation.

37 You, O king, the king of kings, to whom the Creator of heaven has given the kingdom, the power, and the might, and the glory,

38 And into whose hand he has given, wherever they dwell, the children of man, the beasts of the field, and the birds of the heavens, making you rule over them all — you are the head of gold.

39 Another kingdom inferior to you shall arise after you, and yet a third kingdom of bronze, which shall rule over all the earth.

40 And there shall be a fourth kingdom, strong as iron, because iron breaks to pieces and shatters all things. And like iron that crushes, it shall break and crush all these.

41 And as you saw the feet and toes, partly of potter's clay and partly of iron, it shall be a divided kingdom, but some of the firmness of iron shall be in it, just as you saw iron mixed with the soft clay.

42 And as the toes of the feet were partly iron and partly clay, so the kingdom shall be partly strong and partly brittle.

43 As you saw the iron mixed with soft clay, so they will mix with one another in marriage, but they will not hold together, just as iron does not mix with clay.

44 And in the days of those kings the Creator of heaven will set up a kingdom that shall never be destroyed, nor shall the kingdom be left to another people. It shall break in pieces all these kingdoms and bring them to an end, and it shall stand forever,

45 Just as you saw that a stone was cut from a mountain by no human hand, and that it broke in pieces the iron, the bronze, the clay, the silver, and the gold. A great Creator has made known to the king what shall be after this. The dream is certain, and its interpretation sure.

46 Then King Nevukhadnetzar fell upon his face and paid homage to Daniyel, and commanded that an offering and incense be offered up to him.

47 The king answered and said to Daniyel, Truly, your Creator is Creator of creators and Lord of kings, and a revealer of mysteries, for you have been able to reveal this mystery.

48 Then the king gave Daniyel high honors and many great gifts, and made him ruler over the whole province of Babylon and chief prefect over all the wise men of Babylon.

49 Daniyel made a request of the king, and he appointed Shadrakh, Meshakh, and Aved-Nego over the affairs of the province of Babylon. But Daniyel remained at the king's court.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] The Qumran Daniyel manuscripts confirm Chapter 2. The chapter begins in Hebrew — verses 1-4a — then transitions to Aramaic at verse 4b with the Chaldeans' response — and continues in Aramaic through Chapter 7:28. The Aramaic section spans the heart of the court narrative. The four-kingdom vision — head of gold, chest of silver, middle of bronze, legs of iron, feet of mixed iron and clay — is the covenant most cited covenant political covenant prophecy in the covenant canon.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 2 in full. Verse 20-22 — blessed be the name of Creator from everlasting to everlasting, to whom belong wisdom and might. He changes times and seasons. He removes kings and sets up kings. He gives wisdom to the wise. He reveals deep and hidden things. Daniyel's covenant night-prayer after the covenant revelation of the mystery is the covenant theological center of the chapter: the covenant answer to the covenant king's covenant impossible demand comes through covenant prayer and produces covenant praise.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic text preserves Chapter 2 in full. Verse 44 — in the days of those kings the Creator of heaven will set up a kingdom that shall never be destroyed, nor shall the kingdom be left to another people. It shall break in pieces all these kingdoms and bring them to an end, and it shall stand forever. The covenant kingdom that the stone cut without human hands establishes is the covenant eschatological covenant kingdom of Creator — permanent, covenant indestructible, covenant universal — in contrast to every covenant human covenant empire.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 34-35 — a stone was cut out by no human hand, and it struck the image — and broke them in pieces — but the stone that struck the image became a great mountain and filled the whole earth. The covenant stone cut without human hands is the covenant most striking covenant image in the chapter: the covenant kingdom that destroys all covenant human covenant empires is not the product of covenant human covenant military or covenant political covenant effort. It comes from outside the covenant human covenant system — cut from the mountain by no human hand.

DANIYEL — CHAPTER 3 Nevukhadnetzar Made an Image of Gold — Whoever Does Not Fall Down and Worship Shall Be Cast Into the Furnace — We Will Not Serve Your Gods — The Form of the Fourth Is Like a Son of the Gods — Shadrakh Meshakh and Aved-Nego Came Out of the Fire

1 King Nevukhadnetzar made an image of gold, whose height was sixty cubits and its breadth six cubits. He set it up on the plain of Dura, in the province of Babylon.

2 Then King Nevukhadnetzar sent to gather the satraps, the prefects, and the governors, the counselors, the treasurers, the justices, the magistrates, and all the officials of the provinces to come to the dedication of the image that King Nevukhadnetzar had set up.

3 Then the satraps, the prefects, and the governors, the counselors, the treasurers, the justices, the magistrates, and all the officials of the provinces gathered for the dedication of the image that King Nevukhadnetzar had set up. And they stood before the image that Nevukhadnetzar had set up.

4 And the herald proclaimed aloud, You are commanded, O peoples, nations, and languages,

5 That when you hear the sound of the horn, pipe, lyre, trigon, harp, bagpipe, and every kind of music, you are to fall down and worship the golden image that King Nevukhadnetzar has set up.

6 And whoever does not fall down and worship shall immediately be cast into a burning fiery furnace.

7 Therefore, as soon as all the peoples heard the sound of the horn, pipe, lyre, trigon, harp, bagpipe, and every kind of music, all the peoples, nations, and languages fell down and worshiped the golden image that King Nevukhadnetzar had set up.

8 Therefore at that time certain Chaldeans came forward and maliciously accused the Yehudim.

9 They declared to King Nevukhadnetzar, O king, live forever.

10 You, O king, have made a decree, that every man who hears the sound of the horn, pipe, lyre, trigon, harp, bagpipe, and every kind of music, shall fall down and worship the golden image.

11 And whoever does not fall down and worship shall be cast into a burning fiery furnace.

12 There are certain Yehudim whom you have appointed over the affairs of the province of Babylon: Shadrakh, Meshakh, and Aved-Nego. These men, O king, pay no attention to you. They do not serve your gods or worship the golden image that you have set up.

13 Then Nevukhadnetzar in furious rage commanded that Shadrakh, Meshakh, and Aved-Nego be brought. So they brought these men before the king.

14 Nevukhadnetzar answered and said to them, Is it true, O Shadrakh, Meshakh, and Aved-Nego, that you do not serve my gods or worship the golden image that I have set up?

15 Now if you are ready when you hear the sound of the horn, pipe, lyre, trigon, harp, bagpipe, and every kind of music, to fall down and worship the image that I have made, well and good. But if you do not worship, you shall immediately be cast into a burning fiery furnace. And who is the god who will deliver you out of my hands?

16 Shadrakh, Meshakh, and Aved-Nego answered and said to the king, O Nevukhadnetzar, we have no need to answer you in this matter.

17 If this be so, our Creator whom we serve is able to deliver us from the burning fiery furnace, and he will deliver us out of your hand, O king.

18 But if not, be it known to you, O king, that we will not serve your gods or worship the golden image that you have set up.

19 Then Nevukhadnetzar was filled with fury, and the expression of his face was changed against Shadrakh, Meshakh, and Aved-Nego. He ordered the furnace heated seven times more than it was usually heated.

20 And he ordered some of the mighty men of his army to bind Shadrakh, Meshakh, and Aved-Nego, and to cast them into the burning fiery furnace.

21 Then these men were bound in their cloaks, their tunics, their hats, and their other garments, and they were thrown into the burning fiery furnace.

22 Because the king's order was urgent and the furnace overheated, the flame of the fire killed those men who took up Shadrakh, Meshakh, and Aved-Nego.

23 And these three men, Shadrakh, Meshakh, and Aved-Nego, fell bound into the burning fiery furnace.

24 Then King Nevukhadnetzar was astonished and rose up in haste. He declared to his counselors, Did we not cast three men bound into the fire? They answered and said to the king, True, O king.

25 He answered and said, But I see four men unbound, walking in the midst of the fire, and they are not hurt. And the appearance of the fourth is like a son of the gods.

26 Then Nevukhadnetzar came near to the door of the burning fiery furnace. He declared, Shadrakh, Meshakh, and Aved-Nego, servants of the Most High Creator, come out, and come here. Then Shadrakh, Meshakh, and Aved-Nego came out from the fire.

27 And the satraps, the prefects, the governors, and the king's counselors gathered together and saw that the fire had not had any power over the bodies of those men. The hair of their heads was not singed, their cloaks were not harmed, and no smell of fire had come upon them.

28 Nevukhadnetzar answered and said, Blessed be the Creator of Shadrakh, Meshakh, and Aved-Nego, who has sent his angel and delivered his servants, who trusted in him, and set aside the king's command, and yielded up their bodies rather than serve and worship any god except their own Creator.

29 Therefore I make a decree: Any people, nation, or language that speaks anything against the Creator of Shadrakh, Meshakh, and Aved-Nego shall be torn limb from limb, and their houses laid in ruins, for there is no other god who is able to rescue in this way.

30 Then the king promoted Shadrakh, Meshakh, and Aved-Nego in the province of Babylon.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] The Qumran manuscripts confirm Chapter 3 in Aramaic. The fiery furnace narrative is the covenant paradigmatic covenant narrative of covenant faithful covenant resistance to covenant idolatrous covenant compulsion under covenant foreign covenant sovereignty. Verses 17-18 — our Creator whom we serve is able to deliver us — but if not, be it known to you, O king, that we will not serve your gods — is the covenant most concentrated covenant expression of covenant unconditional covenant faithfulness in the covenant book.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 3 in full. Verse 17-18 — our Creator whom we serve is able to deliver us from the burning fiery furnace. But if not, be it known to you, O king, that we will not serve your gods or worship the golden image that you have set up. The covenant but if not — uveh-la — is the covenant most theologically significant covenant phrase in the chapter: the covenant faithfulness of the three covenant men is not contingent on covenant divine covenant deliverance. They will refuse whether delivered or not.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic text preserves Chapter 3 in full. Verse 25 — I see four men unbound, walking in the midst of the fire, and they are not hurt. And the appearance of the fourth is like a son of the gods. Nevukhadnetzar's covenant observation of the fourth figure in the fire is made from outside the furnace — he sees what cannot be explained by covenant natural covenant means. The covenant fourth presence is the covenant divine covenant accompaniment of the covenant faithful in the covenant extremity of their covenant suffering.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 28 — Blessed be the Creator of Shadrakh, Meshakh, and Aved-Nego, who has sent his angel and delivered his servants, who trusted in him, and set aside the king's command, and yielded up their bodies rather than serve and worship any god except their own Creator. Nevukhadnetzar's covenant blessing of the Creator of the three men is the covenant same covenant pattern as the queen of Sheba's covenant blessing of YHWH in Divrei HaYamim Bet 9:8 — the covenant foreign covenant ruler acknowledges the covenant superior power of the covenant community's Creator.

DANIYEL — CHAPTER 4 Nevukhadnetzar to All Peoples — I Saw a Dream That Made Me Afraid — A Watcher a Holy One Came Down — Let His Portion Be With the Beasts — Daniyel Was Dismayed — You Shall Be Driven From Men — Until You Know That the Most High Rules — All This Came Upon Nevukhadnetzar

1 King Nevukhadnetzar to all peoples, nations, and languages that dwell in all the earth: Peace be multiplied to you.

2 It has seemed good to me to show the signs and wonders that the Most High Creator has done for me.

3 How great are his signs, how mighty his wonders. His kingdom is an everlasting kingdom, and his dominion endures from generation to generation.

4 I, Nevukhadnetzar, was at ease in my house and prospering in my palace.

5 I saw a dream that made me afraid. As I lay in bed the fancies and the visions of my head alarmed me.

6 So I made a decree that all the wise men of Babylon should be brought before me, that they might make known to me the interpretation of the dream.

7 Then the magicians, the enchanters, the Chaldeans, and the astrologers came in, and I told them the dream, but they could not make known to me its interpretation.

8 At last Daniyel came in before me — he who was named Belteshazzar after the name of my god, and in whom is the spirit of the holy gods — and I told him the dream, saying,

9 O Belteshazzar, chief of the magicians, because I know that the spirit of the holy gods is in you and that no mystery is too difficult for you, tell me the visions of my dream that I saw and their interpretation.

10 The visions of my head as I lay in bed were these: I saw, and behold, a tree in the midst of the earth, and its height was great.

11 The tree grew and became strong, and its top reached to heaven, and it was visible to the end of the whole earth.

12 Its leaves were beautiful and its fruit abundant, and in it was food for all. The beasts of the field found shade under it, and the birds of the heavens lived in its branches, and all flesh was fed from it.

13 I saw in the visions of my head as I lay in bed, and behold, a watcher, a holy one, came down from heaven.

14 He proclaimed aloud and said thus: Chop down the tree and lop off its branches, strip off its leaves and scatter its fruit. Let the beasts flee from under it and the birds from its branches.

15 But leave the stump of its roots in the earth, bound with a band of iron and bronze, amid the tender grass of the field. Let him be wet with the dew of heaven. Let his portion be with the beasts in the grass of the earth.

16 Let his mind be changed from a man's, and let a beast's mind be given to him. And let seven periods of time pass over him.

17 The sentence is by the decree of the watchers, the decision by the word of the holy ones, to the end that the living may know that the Most High rules the kingdom of men and gives it to whom he will and sets over it the lowliest of men.

18 This dream I, King Nevukhadnetzar, saw. And you, O Belteshazzar, tell me the interpretation, because all the wise men of my kingdom are not able to make known to me the interpretation, but you are able, for the spirit of the holy gods is in you.

19 Then Daniyel, whose name was Belteshazzar, was dismayed for a while, and his thoughts alarmed him. The king answered and said, Belteshazzar, let not the dream or the interpretation alarm you. Belteshazzar answered and said, My lord, may the dream be for those who hate you and its interpretation for your enemies.

20 The tree you saw, which grew and became strong, so that its top reached to heaven, and it was visible to the end of the whole earth,

21 Whose leaves were beautiful and its fruit abundant, and in which was food for all, under which beasts of the field found shade, and in whose branches the birds of the heavens lived —

22 It is you, O king, who have grown and become strong. Your greatness has grown and reaches to heaven, and your dominion to the ends of the earth.

23 And because the king saw a watcher, a holy one, coming down from heaven and saying, Chop down the tree and destroy it, but leave the stump of its roots in the earth, bound with a band of iron and bronze, in the tender grass of the field, and let him be wet with the dew of heaven, and let his portion be with the beasts of the field, till seven periods of time pass over him,

24 This is the interpretation, O king: It is a decree of the Most High, which has come upon my lord the king:

25 You shall be driven from among men, and your dwelling shall be with the beasts of the field. You shall be made to eat grass like an ox, and you shall be wet with the dew of heaven, and seven periods of time shall pass over you, till you know that the Most High rules the kingdom of men and gives it to whom he will.

26 And as it was commanded to leave the stump of the roots of the tree, your kingdom shall be confirmed for you from the time that you know that Heaven rules.

27 Therefore, O king, let my counsel be acceptable to you: break off your sins by practicing righteousness, and your iniquities by showing mercy to the oppressed, that there may perhaps be a lengthening of your prosperity.

28 All this came upon King Nevukhadnetzar.

29 At the end of twelve months he was walking on the roof of the royal palace of Babylon,

30 And the king answered and said, Is not this great Babylon, which I have built by my mighty power as a royal residence and for the glory of my majesty?

31 While the words were still in the king's mouth, there fell a voice from heaven, O King Nevukhadnetzar, to you it is spoken: The kingdom has departed from you,

32 And you shall be driven from among men, and your dwelling shall be with the beasts of the field. And you shall be made to eat grass like an ox, and seven periods of time shall pass over you, until you know that the Most High rules the kingdom of men and gives it to whom he will.

33 Immediately the word was fulfilled against Nevukhadnetzar. He was driven from among men and ate grass like an ox, and his body was wet with the dew of heaven till his hair grew as long as eagles' feathers, and his nails were like birds' claws.

34 At the end of the days I, Nevukhadnetzar, lifted my eyes to heaven, and my reason returned to me, and I blessed the Most High, and praised and honored him who lives forever, for his dominion is an everlasting dominion, and his kingdom endures from generation to generation.

35 All the inhabitants of the earth are accounted as nothing, and he does according to his will among the host of heaven and among the inhabitants of the earth. And none can stay his hand or say to him, What have you done?

36 At the same time my reason returned to me, and for the glory of my kingdom, my majesty and splendor returned to me. My counselors and my lords sought me, and I was established in my kingdom, and still more greatness was added to me.

37 Now I, Nevukhadnetzar, praise and extol and honor the King of heaven, for all his works are right and his ways are just. And those who walk in pride he is able to humble.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] The Qumran manuscripts confirm Chapter 4 in Aramaic. The chapter is unique in the covenant canon: it is formally structured as a royal decree issued by Nevukhadnetzar himself — beginning in verse 1 — and is the covenant only chapter in the covenant Hebrew-Aramaic covenant canon written in the first-person voice of a covenant pagan covenant king. The covenant narrative of Nevukhadnetzar's covenant seven-year covenant madness and covenant restoration is the covenant most extended covenant portrait of covenant divine covenant humbling of covenant human covenant pride in the covenant narrative covenant literature.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 4 in full. Verse 34-37 — at the end of the days I lifted my eyes to heaven, and my reason returned to me, and I blessed the Most High, and praised and honored him who lives forever — all the inhabitants of the earth are accounted as nothing — now I, Nevukhadnetzar, praise and extol and honor the King of heaven, for all his works are right and his ways are just. And those who walk in pride he is able to humble. The covenant foreign covenant king's covenant doxology is the covenant theological climax of the chapter.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic text preserves Chapter 4 in full. Verse 17 — the sentence is by the decree of the watchers, the decision by the word of the holy ones, to the end that the living may know that the Most High rules the kingdom of men and gives it to whom he will and sets over it the lowliest of men. The covenant theological purpose of the covenant king's covenant humbling: not merely covenant personal covenant correction but covenant universal covenant proclamation that the Most High rules the covenant kingdoms of men.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 27 — break off your sins by practicing righteousness, and your iniquities by showing mercy to the oppressed, that there may perhaps be a lengthening of your prosperity. Daniyel's covenant pastoral covenant counsel to the covenant foreign covenant king is the covenant most concrete covenant ethical covenant instruction in the covenant book: the covenant path of covenant repentance that might have prevented the covenant judgment is covenant righteousness and covenant mercy toward the covenant oppressed. Twelve months later — verse 29 — the king's covenant pride rather than covenant repentance produced the covenant judgment.

DANIYEL — CHAPTER 5 King Belshazzar Made a Great Feast — They Drank From the Vessels of the House of Creator — Fingers of a Human Hand Appeared — The Queen Said There Is a Man in Your Kingdom — You Have Praised the Gods of Silver and Gold — That Very Night Belshazzar Was Killed

1 King Belshazzar made a great feast for a thousand of his lords and drank wine in front of the thousand.

2 Belshazzar, when he tasted the wine, commanded that the vessels of gold and of silver that Nevukhadnetzar his father had taken out of the temple in Yerushalayim be brought, that the king and his lords, his wives, and his concubines might drink from them.

3 Then they brought in the golden vessels that had been taken out of the temple, the house of Creator in Yerushalayim, and the king and his lords, his wives, and his concubines drank from them.

4 They drank wine and praised the gods of gold and silver, bronze, iron, wood, and stone.

5 Immediately the fingers of a human hand appeared and wrote on the plaster of the wall of the king's palace, opposite the lampstand. And the king saw the hand as it wrote.

6 Then the king's color changed, and his thoughts alarmed him. His limbs gave way, and his knees knocked together.

7 The king called loudly to bring in the enchanters, the Chaldeans, and the astrologers. The king declared to the wise men of Babylon, Whoever reads this writing, and shows me its interpretation, shall be clothed with purple and have a chain of gold around his neck and shall be the third ruler in the kingdom.

8 Then all the king's wise men came in, but they could not read the writing or make known to the king the interpretation.

9 Then King Belshazzar was greatly alarmed, and his color changed, and his lords were perplexed.

10 The queen, because of the words of the king and his lords, came into the banqueting hall, and the queen declared, O king, live forever. Let not your thoughts alarm you or your color change.

11 There is a man in your kingdom in whom is the spirit of the holy gods. In the days of your father, light and understanding and wisdom like the wisdom of the gods were found in him, and King Nevukhadnetzar, your father — your father the king — made him chief of the magicians, enchanters, Chaldeans, and astrologers,

12 Because an excellent spirit, knowledge, and understanding to interpret dreams, explain riddles, and solve problems were found in this Daniyel, whom the king named Belteshazzar. Now let Daniyel be called, and he will show the interpretation.

13 Then Daniyel was brought in before the king. The king answered and said to Daniyel, You are that Daniyel, one of the exiles of Yehudah, whom the king my father brought from Yehudah.

14 I have heard of you that the spirit of the gods is in you, and that light and understanding and excellent wisdom are found in you.

15 Now the wise men, the enchanters, have been brought in before me to read this writing and make known to me its interpretation, but they could not show the interpretation of the matter.

16 But I have heard that you can give interpretations and solve problems. Now if you can read the writing and make known to me its interpretation, you shall be clothed with purple and have a chain of gold around your neck and shall be the third ruler in the kingdom.

17 Then Daniyel answered and said before the king, Let your gifts be for yourself, and give your rewards to another. Nevertheless, I will read the writing to the king and make known to him the interpretation.

18 O king, the Most High Creator gave Nevukhadnetzar your father kingship and greatness and glory and majesty.

19 And because of the greatness that he gave him, all peoples, nations, and languages trembled and feared before him. Whom he would, he killed, and whom he would, he kept alive. Whom he would, he raised up, and whom he would, he humbled.

20 But when his heart was lifted up and his spirit was hardened so that he dealt proudly, he was brought down from his kingly throne, and his glory was taken from him.

21 He was driven from among the children of mankind, and his mind was made like that of a beast, and his dwelling was with the wild donkeys. He was fed grass like an ox, and his body was wet with the dew of heaven, until he knew that the Most High Creator rules the kingdom of mankind and sets over it whom he will.

22 And you his son, Belshazzar, have not humbled your heart, though you knew all this,

23 But you have lifted up yourself against the Lord of heaven. And the vessels of his house have been brought in before you, and you and your lords, your wives, and your concubines have drunk wine from them. And you have praised the gods of silver and gold, of bronze, iron, wood, and stone, which do not see or hear or know, but the Creator in whose hand is your breath, and whose are all your ways, you have not honored.

24 Then from his presence the hand was sent, and this writing was inscribed.

25 And this is the writing that was inscribed: MENE, MENE, TEKEL, and PARSIN.

26 This is the interpretation of the matter: MENE, Creator has numbered the days of your kingdom and brought it to an end.

27 TEKEL, you have been weighed in the balances and found wanting.

28 PERES, your kingdom is divided and given to the Medes and Persians.

29 Then Belshazzar gave the command, and Daniyel was clothed with purple, a chain of gold was put around his neck, and a proclamation was made about him, that he should be the third ruler in the kingdom.

30 That very night Belshazzar the Chaldean king was killed.

31 And Darius the Mede received the kingdom, being about sixty-two years old.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] The Qumran manuscripts confirm Chapter 5 in Aramaic. The handwriting on the wall is the covenant most dramatic covenant single covenant moment in the Daniyel court narratives: the covenant mysterious covenant script on the wall — MENE, MENE, TEKEL, PARSIN — is addressed to Belshazzar who used the covenant sacred vessels of the covenant temple of YHWH for covenant idolatrous covenant drinking. The covenant desecration of the covenant temple vessels is the covenant specific covenant act that triggers the covenant judgment.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 5 in full. Verse 22-23 — you his son, Belshazzar, have not humbled your heart, though you knew all this, but you have lifted up yourself against the Lord of heaven. And the vessels of his house have been brought in before you — and the Creator in whose hand is your breath, and whose are all your ways, you have not honored. Daniyel's covenant indictment of Belshazzar is the covenant sharpest covenant prophetic covenant rebuke in the covenant book: Belshazzar had the example of Nevukhadnetzar and still chose covenant pride.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic text preserves Chapter 5 in full. Verse 27 — TEKEL, you have been weighed in the balances and found wanting. The covenant most concise covenant judgment in the covenant book: the covenant royal covenant life weighed in covenant divine covenant scales and found covenant short of covenant required covenant weight. The covenant weighing metaphor is the covenant ancient Near Eastern covenant judgment imagery — the covenant same covenant scales that appear in the covenant Egyptian covenant Book of the Dead — here applied to a covenant living covenant king's covenant entire covenant reign.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 17 — let your gifts be for yourself, and give your rewards to another. Nevertheless, I will read the writing to the king and make known to him the interpretation. Daniyel's covenant refusal of the covenant royal covenant reward before delivering the covenant interpretation is the covenant mark of the covenant authentic covenant prophetic covenant voice: the covenant message is not for covenant sale. The covenant interpretation will be given because Creator commands covenant truth-speaking to covenant power, not because covenant power offers covenant compensation.

DANIYEL — CHAPTER 6 Darius Set Over the Kingdom — The Administrators Sought to Find a Fault in Daniyel — We Shall Not Find Any Ground Unless We Find It in the Law of His Creator — Daniyel Was Thrown Into the Lions' Den — Your Creator Whom You Serve Continually Will Deliver You — Creator Sent His Angel

1 It pleased Darius to set over the kingdom 120 satraps, to be throughout the whole kingdom.

2 And over them three high officials, of whom Daniyel was one, to whom these satraps should give account, so that the king might suffer no loss.

3 Then this Daniyel became distinguished above all the other high officials and satraps, because an excellent spirit was in him. And the king planned to set him over the whole kingdom.

4 Then the high officials and the satraps sought to find a ground for complaint against Daniyel with regard to the kingdom, but they could find no ground for complaint or any fault, because he was faithful, and no error or fault was found in him.

5 Then these men said, We shall not find any ground for complaint against this Daniyel unless we find it in connection with the law of his Creator.

6 Then these high officials and satraps came by agreement to the king and said to him, O King Darius, live forever.

7 All the high officials of the kingdom, the prefects and the satraps, the counselors and the governors are agreed that the king should establish an ordinance and enforce an injunction, that whoever makes petition to any god or man for thirty days, except to you, O king, shall be cast into the den of lions.

8 Now, O king, establish the injunction and sign the document, so that it cannot be changed, according to the law of the Medes and the Persians, which cannot be revoked.

9 Therefore King Darius signed the document and injunction.

10 When Daniyel knew that the document had been signed, he went to his house where he had windows in his upper chamber open toward Yerushalayim. He got down on his knees three times a day and prayed and gave thanks before his Creator, as he had done previously.

11 Then these men came by agreement and found Daniyel making petition and plea before his Creator.

12 Then they came near and said before the king, concerning the injunction, O king, did you not sign an injunction, that anyone who makes petition to any god or man within thirty days except to you, O king, shall be cast into the den of lions? The king answered and said, The thing stands fast, according to the law of the Medes and Persians, which cannot be revoked.

13 Then they answered and said before the king, Daniyel, who is one of the exiles from Yehudah, pays no attention to you, O king, or the injunction you have signed, but makes his petition three times a day.

14 Then the king, when he heard these words, was much distressed and set his mind to deliver Daniyel. And he labored till the sun went down to rescue him.

15 Then these men came by agreement to the king and said to the king, Know, O king, that it is a law of the Medes and Persians that no injunction or ordinance that the king establishes can be changed.

16 Then the king commanded, and Daniyel was brought and cast into the den of lions. The king declared to Daniyel, May your Creator, whom you serve continually, deliver you.

17 And a stone was brought and laid on the mouth of the den, and the king sealed it with his own signet and with the signet of his lords, that nothing might be changed concerning Daniyel.

18 Then the king went to his palace and spent the night fasting. No diversions were brought to him, and sleep fled from him.

19 Then, at break of day, the king arose and went in haste to the den of lions.

20 As he came near to the den where Daniyel was, he cried out in a tone of anguish. The king declared to Daniyel, O Daniyel, servant of the living Creator, has your Creator, whom you serve continually, been able to deliver you from the lions?

21 Then Daniyel said to the king, O king, live forever.

22 My Creator sent his angel and shut the lions' mouths, and they have not harmed me, because I was found blameless before him. And also before you, O king, I have done no harm.

23 Then the king was exceedingly glad, and commanded that Daniyel be taken up out of the den. So Daniyel was taken up out of the den, and no kind of harm was found on him, because he had trusted in his Creator.

24 And the king commanded, and those men who had maliciously accused Daniyel were brought and cast into the den of lions — they, their children, and their wives. And before they reached the bottom of the den, the lions overpowered them and broke all their bones in pieces.

25 Then King Darius wrote to all the peoples, nations, and languages that dwell in all the earth: Peace be multiplied to you.

26 I make a decree, that in all my royal dominion people are to tremble and fear before the Creator of Daniyel, for he is the living Creator, enduring forever. His kingdom shall never be destroyed, and his dominion shall be to the end.

27 He delivers and rescues. He works signs and wonders in heaven and on earth, he who has saved Daniyel from the power of the lions.

28 So this Daniyel prospered during the reign of Darius and the reign of Koresh the Persian.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] The Qumran manuscripts confirm Chapter 6 in Aramaic. The lions' den narrative is the structural counterpart in the Median-Persian court to the fiery furnace narrative in the Babylonian court: in both cases the covenant faithful refuse covenant idolatrous covenant compliance, in both cases they are condemned to death, in both cases they are delivered by the Creator, and in both cases the covenant foreign covenant king issues a proclamation acknowledging the covenant superior power of the Creator of the covenant faithful.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 6 in full. Verse 10 — when Daniyel knew that the document had been signed, he went to his house where he had windows in his upper chamber open toward Yerushalayim. He got down on his knees three times a day and prayed and gave thanks before his Creator, as he had done previously. The covenant unchanging covenant prayer practice of Daniyel: the royal covenant edict does not alter his covenant posture before Creator. The covenant windows toward Yerushalayim orient his covenant prayer toward the covenant city of the covenant covenant temple.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic text preserves Chapter 6 in full. Verse 26-27 — I make a decree, that in all my royal dominion people are to tremble and fear before the Creator of Daniyel, for he is the living Creator, enduring forever. His kingdom shall never be destroyed, and his dominion shall be to the end. He delivers and rescues. He works signs and wonders in heaven and on earth. Darius's decree echoes the covenant structure of Nevukhadnetzar's decrees in Chapters 3 and 4: the covenant foreign covenant kings are repeatedly brought to covenant acknowledgment of the Creator's covenant sovereign power.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 4-5 — they could find no ground for complaint or any fault, because he was faithful, and no error or fault was found in him. Then these men said, We shall not find any ground for complaint against this Daniyel unless we find it in connection with the law of his Creator. The covenant adversaries' acknowledgment of Daniyel's covenant blameless covenant conduct is the covenant highest covenant possible covenant commendation of the covenant book's covenant central covenant character: his covenant enemies are forced to concede that his only vulnerability is his covenant faithfulness itself.

END OF PART ONE — CHAPTERS 1 THROUGH 6
